After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.

Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after pressing RIGHT. Looking at the image, the right handle on the slider bar seems to have moved - the blue/sky blue handle on the right side of the bar shifted. Let me compare the before and after frames to see exactly what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after to see what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

# Find differences in node positions
before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after['nodes']}

print("Changes detected:")
for nid in before_nodes:
    bn = before_nodes[nid]
    an = after_nodes.get(nid)
    if an and bn['boundary'] != an['boundary']:
        print(f"  Node {nid} (color={bn['color']}): boundary changed")
        print(f"    before: {bn['boundary']}")
        print(f"    after:  {an['boundary']}")
    elif an and bn['hash'] != an['hash']:
        print(f"  Node {nid}: hash changed {bn['hash']} -> {an['hash']}")

# Also check for new/removed nodes
before_ids = set(before_nodes.keys())
after_ids = set(after_nodes.keys())
if before_ids != after_ids:
    print(f"  Nodes added: {after_ids - before_ids}")
    print(f"  Nodes removed: {before_ids - after_ids}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Changes detected:
  Node 5: hash changed 1fd5a6753462fbd0 -> 9059581ee351aa20
  Node 6: hash changed 9059581ee351aa20 -> 1fd5a6753462fbd0

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The hashes of nodes 5 and 6 swapped. Node 5 was blue (b) and node 6 was sky blue (S). After pressing RIGHT, their hashes swapped, which means they exchanged positions. The right handle on the slider moved - the blue and sky blue parts swapped.
So RIGHT moves the right handle. Let me check if LEFT moves it back, and also test if the handles can move independently. Let me also try to understand what the goal is - maybe I need to align the handles with something.
Let me try LEFT to see if it reverses the change, and then try other directions to understand the full control scheme.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# RIGHT swapped the right handle's blue and sky-blue parts
# Let me try LEFT to see if it reverses
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
